FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: N=8,446 adolescents from 2016-2018 baseline with 2 years of follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is 'neighborhood quality measures' (COI, SVI, ADI) via geospatial data. Outcome is 'total screen time' generic composite with no smartphone/SM-specific breakdown. Abstract specifies 'television/movie watching, video game playing, and video streaming' but these are grouped in aggregate 'total screen time' measure only.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 fails; paper does not separately report smartphone/SM-specific coefficient

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT-IC2 (generic screen time composite with no phone/SM breakdown); does not proceed to EC7 evaluation
notes: Paper studies neighborhood quality predictors of screen time. Outcome is aggregate total screen time composite (tv/movies, video games, streaming) without smartphone or social media specific measures extractable. Excluded per FT-IC2 NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: N=8,119 participants Year 2 (2018-2020; ages 11-12), with Year 3 follow-up outcomes. ABCD Study 5.0 release.

FT_IC2.evidence: Three specific smartphone/SM problematic use scales: (1) Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (8 items, range 1-7), (2) Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (6 items, range 1-6 per Bergen Facebook scale), (3) Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (6 items, range 1-6 per Bergen scale). All are problematic-use addiction-like scales measuring behavioral patterns.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear and logistic regression models: Problematic phone and SM use prospectively associated with CBCL DSM-oriented scales (continuous); suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, substance initiation (binary outcomes). Figures 1 and 2 report forest plots of coefficients and AORs with 95% CIs.

FT_IC4.evidence: Coefficients reported in Figures 1 and 2 forest plots with 95% CIs and p-values. Abstract states 'B = 0.39-0.64' range examples; text reports linear regression coefficients and AORs. Appendix Table 3 available online with full numeric results.

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective cohort analysis: exposures measured Year 2 (ages 11-12), outcomes assessed Year 3 (1-year follow-up). Uses linear and logistic regression with covariates; not measurement-only.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (American Journal of Preventive Medicine, 2026)

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone/SM are independent variables (predictors) of mental health and substance use outcomes, not dependent variables. EC7 does not apply. Paper reports phone/SM → non-SM outcomes (depression, sleep, substance use).
notes: High-quality prospective ABCD analysis. Problematic phone, SM, and video game use predict multiple mental health and behavioral outcomes 1 year later. Clear numerical results in figures.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: N=7,528 prospective cohort, baseline ages 8-13 years (2016-2018) to Year 2 follow-up (2018-2020). ABCD 5.1 release.

FT_IC2.evidence: Independent variable: youth-reported daily social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey at baseline, Year 1, Year 2. Platforms specified: Instagram, Facebook, Twitter. Weighted average hours/day calculated from weekday/weekend reports. Specific to social media (not generic screen time).

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models: social media time trajectories (3 groups: no/very low, low-increasing, high-increasing) associated with cognitive outcomes (Little Man Task accuracy, Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test performance at Year 2). Adjusted for baseline cognition, age, sex, race, ethnicity, income, parental education, ADHD symptoms, depression, non-SM screen use, site.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients with 95% CIs reported: RAVLT Initial Trials (B: -1.38; 95% CI: -1.82, -0.94), RAVLT Retroactive Interference (B: -0.38; 95% CI: -0.52, -0.25), RAVLT Long Delay (B: -0.41; 95% CI: -0.55, -0.26), LMT accuracy (B: -0.03; 95% CI: -0.05, -0.01). All in abstract and main text.

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective cohort with trajectory-based modeling: social media use patterns baseline-Year 2, cognitive outcomes measured Year 2. Original empirical analysis using group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) and linear regression.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Lancet Regional Health - Americas, 2026)

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is independent variable predicting cognitive memory outcomes (Little Man Task, RAVLT), not dependent variable. Paper reports SM → cognitive performance associations.
notes: High-quality prospective study using trajectory modeling. Social media use trajectories prospectively associated with lower verbal and spatial memory performance 2 years later. All coefficients with 95% CIs provided.
